factors.summary: Capital expenditures are expected to increase in 2022, primarily driven by aircraft purchases and airport construction projects.

evidence.text: Our expected 2022 capital expenditures of approximately $6.0 billion, which may vary depending on financing decisions, will be primarily for aircraft, including deliveries and advance deposit payments, as well as fleet modifications and technology enhancements.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Expected increase in capital expenditures for growth investments.

factors.summary: The company is deleveraging by repaying debt early and through scheduled amortization, which is expected to continue in 2022.

evidence.text: We will continue to seek opportunities to pre-pay our debt, in addition to periodic amortization payments and scheduled maturities, during 2022 and beyond.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Company is deleveraging through debt repayment, improving financial health.

factors.summary: The imposition of tariffs on aircraft could significantly increase costs and impact financial results.

evidence.text: The imposition of significant tariffs with respect to aircraft that we are not able to mitigate could substantially increase our costs, which in turn could have a material adverse effect on our financial results.
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is subject to extensive government regulation, which is costly and could materially adversely affect our business.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Potential significant cost increase from tariffs poses major risk.

factors.summary: Talent retention is a challenge, particularly with pilots, as there is a shortage of qualified personnel.

evidence.text: Some regional carriers, including our wholly-owned subsidiary, Endeavor, have faced a shortage of qualified pilots. If this shortage becomes more widespread, third-party regional carriers may not be able to comply with their obligations to us, and Endeavor may not be able to perform as expected, which could reduce our capacity (available seat miles) and revenue, resulting in a material adverse effect on our business and results of operations.
evidence.subsection: Our commercial relationships with airlines in other parts of the world and the investments that we have in certain of those carriers may not produce the results or returns we expect.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Pilot shortage poses a challenge to operations and financial performance.

factors.summary: Digital automation is a focus area, with investments in customer-facing technology and cloud-based infrastructure, though there are associated risks of disruption.

evidence.text: We are investing in significant upgrades to technology infrastructure and other supporting systems and transitioning to cloud-based technologies. The performance, reliability and security of the technology we use are critical to our ability to serve customers.
evidence.subsection: Failure of the technology we use to perform effectively could have a material adverse effect on our business.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Investments in technology infrastructure and cloud-based systems enhance capabilities.

factors.summary: Delta's order backlog is decreasing due to aircraft sales.

evidence.text: Following the impairment charges, the aggregate net book value of these aircraft as of December 31, 2021 and December 31, 2020 was approximately $340 million and $500 million, respectively, with the reduction in 2021 primarily due to aircraft sales.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Decrease in order backlog due to aircraft sales may indicate reduced demand.

factors.summary: Delta's inventory levels are normalizing with the return of aircraft to service.

evidence.text: Due to the recovery in demand that we have experienced throughout 2021, we decided not to retire any additional aircraft and returned to service a majority of the aircraft that were temporarily parked in 2020.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Normalization of inventory levels suggests efficient operations post-pandemic recovery.

factors.summary: Delta has not reported significant quality or safety issues.

evidence.text: No specific incidents or recalls are mentioned in the provided text.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: No significant quality or safety issues reported, indicating strong performance.

factors.summary: Delta's forward guidance is cautious due to the uncertain recovery from the pandemic.

evidence.text: Given the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on customer behavior and changes made in ticket validity terms, as well as the elimination of change fees for most tickets, our estimates of revenue that will be recognized from the air traffic liability for unused tickets may vary in future periods.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Forward guidance is cautious due to the uncertain recovery from the pandemic.

factors.summary: Management is cautiously optimistic, with actions like extending ticket validity periods indicating flexibility.

evidence.text: In the March 2021 quarter, we announced the extension of the validity of all passenger tickets and travel credits purchased or expiring in 2021 to December 31, 2022, which allowed for tickets to be rebooked through December 31, 2022 for travel through 2023. In January 2022, we announced that all existing travel credit holders will have until December 31, 2023 to rebook their ticket for travel throughout 2024. Additionally, all Delta customers with upcoming 2022 travel or who purchase a ticket in 2022 will also have the flexibility to rebook their ticket through December 31, 2023, and travel throughout 2024.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Management is cautiously optimistic with actions showing flexibility and adaptability.

factors.summary: Near-term risks include potential future impairments and the impact of fluctuating fuel prices.

evidence.text: As we gained updated information during the year, we updated estimates to the 2020 fleet-related impairment charges and recorded adjustments of $19 million to certain of the restructuring charges during 2021.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Near-term risks include potential impairments and fuel price fluctuations, indicating challenges.

factors.summary: Delta's long-term strategy is focused on recovery and sustainability post-pandemic.

evidence.text: Due to the recovery in demand that we have experienced throughout 2021, we decided not to retire any additional aircraft and returned to service a majority of the aircraft that were temporarily parked in 2020.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Long-term strategy focuses on recovery and sustainability post-pandemic, showing resilience.

factors.summary: Delta's recovery and return of aircraft to service are catalysts for improved results.

evidence.text: Due to the recovery in demand that we have experienced throughout 2021, we decided not to retire any additional aircraft and returned to service a majority of the aircraft that were temporarily parked in 2020.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Recovery and aircraft return to service are catalysts for improved financial results.

factors.summary: Geopolitical risks, including terrorist attacks and conflicts, pose a significant threat to Delta's operations.

evidence.text: Terrorist attacks, geopolitical conflict or security events, or the fear or threat of any of these events, could have a significant adverse effect on our business.
evidence.subsection: Terrorist attacks, geopolitical conflict or security events may adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Geopolitical risks pose a significant threat to operations, indicating external vulnerabilities.

factors.summary: Delta's sustainability goals and environmental initiatives are subject to external pressures and pose reputational risks.

evidence.text: Significant damage to our reputation and brand, including as a result of significant adverse publicity or inability to achieve certain sustainability goals, could materially adversely affect our business and financial results.
evidence.subsection: Significant extended disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel, including from Monroe, could have a material adverse effect on our operations and results of operations.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Sustainability goals face external pressures and reputational risks, but no clear signal on progress.

factors.summary: Delta's route network is facing challenges due to geopolitical conflicts and disruptions.

evidence.text: The impact on our operations of avoiding areas of the world, including airspace, in which there are geopolitical conflicts and the targeting of commercial aircraft by parties to those conflicts can be significant.
evidence.subsection: Terrorist attacks, geopolitical conflict or security events may adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Geopolitical conflicts and disruptions challenge the route network, indicating operational difficulties.

factors.summary: Demand trends are showing mixed signs of recovery. While there are positive indicators such as increased refinery operations, higher operating revenues, and improved domestic demand, there are ongoing challenges from COVID-19 variants that have moderated demand and affected advance bookings.

evidence.text: During 2021, the refinery progressively increased operations, ending the year at near pre-pandemic levels.

Domestic demand has improved since the latter half of the March 2021 quarter as consumers have regained confidence to travel and increased ticket purchases for travel further in advance. However, the reduction in demand for air travel due to the COVID-19 pandemic resulted in a lower level of advance bookings and the associated cash received than we have historically experienced which has impacted the typical seasonal trend of air traffic liability since March 2020.

Operating revenue increased $12.8 billion, or 75%, compared to the year ended December 31, 2020 due primarily to increased demand in 2021 as a result of the continued recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic.

factors.summary: The company's revenue mix has shifted towards higher-margin third-party refinery sales and higher-margin domestic and international passenger revenues, contributing to overall growth. However, there is evidence of changes in ticket breakage and travel credits impacting the revenue mix, with travel credits decreasing from 65% to 45% as of December 31, 2021.

evidence.text: Refinery revenues increased from $3.1 billion in 2020 to $6.1 billion in 2021, primarily driven by the increase in third-party refinery sales.
Domestic passenger unit revenue ('PRASM') for the year ended December 31, 2021 increased 27% with capacity up 45% compared to the year ended December 31, 2020...

Travel credits represented approximately 45% of the air traffic liability as of December 31, 2021. This compares to approximately 65% as of December 31, 2020 and approximately 20% prior to the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic.

factors.summary: Delta's operating margins show a mixed trajectory with some improvements and pressures. While the refinery segment saw a significant decrease in its operating loss from 2020 to 2021, other areas face challenges such as rising fuel and labor costs, increased RINs compliance costs, and potential impairments from intangible assets. Additionally, the ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic, including ticket breakage and reduced revenue, further complicate the margin situation.

evidence.text: The refinery decreased its operating loss from $216 million in 2020 to $2 million in 2021 mainly due to the increased production and pricing, partially offset by higher Renewable Identification Numbers ('RINs') compliance costs discussed below.
Total operating expense increased slightly compared to 2020, with fuel costs and labor expenses being primary drivers.
Further impairment charges could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations.

Our ticket breakage estimates are primarily based on historical experience, ticket contract terms and customers’ travel behavior. Given the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on customer behavior and changes made in ticket validity terms, as well as the elimination of change fees for most tickets, our estimates of revenue that will be recognized from the air traffic liability for unused tickets may vary in future periods.

factors.summary: Input costs are rising across multiple areas, including fuel prices, RINs compliance costs, and carbon offset expenses. These increases are driven by higher consumption, market price fluctuations, and regulatory compliance, which are expected to continue and potentially worsen. The rising costs are significantly impacting the company's financial results and operational margins.

evidence.text: Monroe incurred $422 million in RINs compliance costs during 2021, of which $98 million related to accrual rate increases on the prior year obligation, in addition to $172 million accrued in 2020.

The average fuel price per gallon increased in 2021. As capacity and demand increased throughout the year, fuel consumption was higher in 2021 than 2020 as well. We expect that fuel consumption will continue to increase throughout 2022 as we return closer to pre-pandemic levels of demand for air travel, partially offset by increases in fuel efficiency of our fleet.
The cost of carbon offsets increased significantly during 2021 and will likely continue to do so, which could adversely affect our financial results.
Fuel expense increased $2.5 billion compared to 2020 primarily due to a 44% increase in consumption on a 45% increase in capacity, and a 31% increase in the market price of jet fuel.

factors.summary: Delta's capacity load yield showed mixed performance in 2021, with load factors improving from 55% to 69%, indicating stronger demand. However, TRASM and fuel efficiency metrics suggest ongoing challenges related to the pandemic's impact on yield and operational efficiency. Additionally, potential disruptions in fuel supply could further negatively affect capacity load yield.

evidence.text: Passenger load factor 69 % 55 % 86 %

As capacity and demand increased throughout the year, fuel consumption was higher in 2021 than 2020 as well. We expect that fuel consumption will continue to increase throughout 2022 as we return closer to pre-pandemic levels of demand for air travel, partially offset by increases in fuel efficiency of our fleet.
TRASM, adjusted 13.71 ¢ 11.87 ¢ 16.97 ¢

Extended interruptions or disruptions in service at major airports in which we operate or significant problems associated with a type of aircraft or engine we operate could have a material adverse effect on our operations.
evidence.subsection: Significant extended disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel, including from Monroe, could have a material adverse effect on our operations and results of operations.

factors.summary: The company's fuel costs are stable compared to 2020, but rising fuel costs pose a risk due to market volatility and insufficient hedging, which could lead to margin pressure. Additionally, challenges with fuel supply and regulatory requirements may impact results of operations.

evidence.text: Average price per fuel gallon $ 2.02 $ 1.64 $ 2.02

High fuel costs or cost increases, including in the cost of crude oil, could have a material adverse effect on our results of operations. We acquire a significant amount of jet fuel from Monroe and through strategic agreements associated with the refinery that Monroe has with third parties. The cost of the fuel we purchase under these arrangements remains subject to volatility in the cost of crude oil and jet fuel. In addition, we have historically purchased a significant amount of aircraft fuel in addition to what we obtain from Monroe. Our aircraft fuel purchase contracts alone do not provide material protection against price increases as these contracts typically establish the price based on industry standard market price indices.
evidence.subsection: Our results can fluctuate due to the effects of weather, natural disasters and seasonality.

evidence.text: Monroe's purchase of RINs at elevated prices in the future could have a material impact on our consolidated results of operations and cash flows.
evidence.subsection: Significant extended disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel, including from Monroe, could have a material adverse effect on our operations and results of operations.

factors.summary: Free cash flow has shown improvement, particularly in 2021, but it remains below pre-pandemic levels as seen in 2019. The company's liquidity position is strong with significant cash and short-term investments, and they expect continued positive cash flows from operations in 2022. However, the free cash flow figure for 2021 is notably lower than 2019, indicating a recovery that is still ongoing.

evidence.text: As of December 31, 2021, we had $14.2 billion in cash, cash equivalents, short-term investments and aggregate principal amount committed and available to be drawn under our revolving credit facilities.
We expect to continue generating positive cash flows from operations during 2022.
These results generated $1.3 billion of free cash flow in 2021 compared to $4.2 billion in 2019.

factors.summary: Delta's unit volumes are growing, supported by increased capacity and demand recovery, particularly in domestic leisure travel. However, the company faces risks from the ongoing impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, including temporary moderations due to variants, and potential negative impacts from geopolitical risks and weather disruptions.

evidence.text: Domestic demand has improved since the latter half of the March 2021 quarter as consumers have regained confidence to travel and increased ticket purchases for travel further in advance.

We expect domestic leisure travel to exceed 2019 levels in 2022, while we expect business travel to continue to return as many companies are expected to expand 'return to office' plans throughout 2022.
The increase in operating revenue, on a 45% increase in capacity, generated a 21% increase in total revenue per available seat mile ('TRASM') and a 16% increase in TRASM, adjusted compared to 2020.

These effects related to the COVID-19 pandemic have negatively impacted air travel in general, which in turn has materially adversely affected our revenues, results of operations and financial condition.

Our results of operations are impacted by severe weather, natural disasters and seasonality. Severe weather conditions and natural disasters (or other environmental events) can significantly disrupt service and create air traffic control problems. These events decrease revenue and can also increase costs.
evidence.subsection: Our results can fluctuate due to the effects of weather, natural disasters and seasonality.

evidence.text: Terrorist attacks, geopolitical conflict or security events, or the fear or threat of any of these events, could have a significant adverse effect on our business.

factors.summary: The travel demand mix is shifting with leisure travel becoming a larger component, leading the recovery, while business travel is expected to recover more slowly. However, external risks such as terrorist attacks or geopolitical conflicts could further negatively impact the demand for air travel, affecting both leisure and business travel.

evidence.text: The mix of business vs. leisure travel is shifting, with leisure travel likely being a larger component due to the pandemic's impact on business travel.
Leisure travel demand is leading the recovery, with business travel expected to recover more slowly.
The rapid spread of the COVID-19 virus... have had, and will continue to have, a material adverse effect on the demand for worldwide air travel compared to historical levels...
Terrorist attacks, geopolitical conflict or security events, or the fear or threat of any of these events, could have a significant adverse effect on our business.
evidence.subsection: Terrorist attacks, geopolitical conflict or security events may adversely affect our business, financial condition and results of operations.

factors.summary: Delta's fleet strategy focuses on fuel efficiency and sustainability, particularly through investments in more fuel-efficient aircraft and the potential use of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF). However, the strategy faces challenges, including the limited commercial viability of SAF due to high costs and reliance on government policies and incentives. Additionally, the fleet strategy is vulnerable to operational disruptions in fuel supply, which could have a material adverse effect on operations and financial results.

evidence.text: Aircraft fleet renewal is an important component of our environmental sustainability strategy and the path to achievement of our ambitious climate goals, which will continue to require extensive capital investment in future periods. While we do not expect a material adverse effect on our Consolidated Financial Statements in the near-term from the use of SAF, we are unable to predict the financial impact of increased use of SAF on our Consolidated Financial Statements over the longer term as government policies and incentives for, and sufficient third-party investment in, SAF are necessary to make its use in larger quantities commercially and economically feasible.
Significant extended disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel, including from Monroe, could have a material adverse effect on our operations and results of operations.
evidence.subsection: Significant extended disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel, including from Monroe, could have a material adverse effect on our operations and results of operations.

factors.summary: Labor costs are a mixed factor for Delta, with some cost reduction measures and manageable expenses in certain periods, but also showing increases due to restored work hours and hiring, as well as concerns about wage inflation and pilot shortages.

evidence.text: Total operating expense, adjusted, decreased $10.9 billion, or 27% compared to 2019, primarily resulting from recognition of the grants from PSP2 and PSP3, lower volume-related expenses (mainly fuel and passenger commissions and other selling expenses), lower salaries and related costs and profit sharing expense, and significant cost reduction measures taken across all aspects of our operation in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Salaries and related costs increased by 8% compared to 2020 as employees were restored to full work hours and hiring increased to support operations.
During 2020, we recorded restructuring charges of $8.2 billion for items such as fleet impairments and voluntary early retirement and separation programs...

evidence.subsection: Our commercial relationships with airlines in other parts of the world and the investments that we have in certain of those carriers may not produce the results or returns we expect.

factors.summary: Delta's supply chain health is showing improvement, particularly with the return of aircraft to service and increased customer confidence due to vaccine distribution. However, there are ongoing concerns related to potential disruptions in third-party technology infrastructure and fuel supply, which could pose risks to operations.

evidence.text: The length and severity of the reduction in travel demand due to the COVID-19 pandemic remains uncertain; however, with continued distribution of effective vaccines and easing of travel advisories and restrictions, we believe customer confidence will continue to grow, leading to increased demand during 2022.

While we have in place initiatives to prevent disruptions and disaster recovery plans and continue to invest in improvements to these initiatives and plans, we have previously experienced infrastructure disruptions and these measures may not be adequate to prevent a future business disruption and any material adverse financial and reputational consequences to our business as recent outages of large cloud providers whom we rely on has shown.
evidence.subsection: Disruptions of our information technology infrastructure could interfere with our operations, possibly having a material adverse effect on our business.

factors.summary: Delta has implemented cost reduction measures and restructuring programs in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, resulting in significant savings. However, these savings have been partially offset by restructuring charges and early debt repayments, indicating a mixed impact on cost management.

evidence.text: Total operating expense decreased $12.4 billion, or 31%, compared to 2019, primarily resulting from recognition of the grants from PSP2 and PSP3, lower volume-related expenses (mainly fuel and passenger commissions and other selling expenses), lower salaries and related costs and profit sharing expense, and significant cost reduction measures taken across all aspects of our operation in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Restructuring charges of $8.2 billion were recorded in 2020, and in 2021, the company made early repayments on debt, reducing interest expense.

During 2020, we recorded restructuring charges of $8.2 billion for items such as fleet impairments and voluntary early retirement and separation programs following strategic business decisions in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.

As a result of the COVID-19 pandemic and our response, we made decisions to remove certain aircraft from active service and to early retire certain fleets. We evaluated our fleet during 2020 and determined that only the fleet types discussed in Note 15 of the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements were impaired, as the future cash flows from the operation of other fleet types through the respective retirement dates exceeded the carrying value. This resulted in impairment and other related charges of $4.4 billion, recorded in restructuring charges in our income statement.

evidence.text: We are planning for our system capacity to be approximately 15% lower in the March 2022 quarter than the March 2019 quarter and approximately 10% lower for the full year of 2022 compared to 2019.

factors.summary: The company faces significant environmental compliance costs and challenges due to regulations aimed at reducing emissions and addressing climate change. The evidence highlights substantial expenses related to carbon offsets and the potential for increased regulatory scrutiny, indicating a notable financial and operational burden.

evidence.text: During 2021, we incurred $95 million of expense related to carbon offsets. This amount consists of $30 million to address 13 million metric tons of carbon emissions generated by our airline segment from March 1 to December 31, 2020 through carbon offsets, as well as an additional $65 million for the purchase and retirement of carbon offsets related to a portion of our airline segment's 2021 carbon emissions.
The airline industry is subject to many forms of environmental regulation, including but not limited to increased regulation to reduce emissions and other risks associated with climate change.

The operation of the refinery by Monroe is subject to significant environmental regulation. Failure to comply with environmental regulations or the enactment of additional regulation applicable to Monroe could have a material adverse effect on our consolidated financial results.

factors.summary: The company faces significant litigation exposure across multiple areas, including potential non-compliance with international laws such as the FCPA, cybersecurity incidents, environmental incidents, and regulatory issues. These risks could result in legal claims, penalties, reputational damage, and financial losses, which could materially adversely affect the company's business and financial results.

evidence.text: Failure to comply with trade sanctions and restrictions, the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act... could result in litigation, assessment of damages, imposition of penalties or other consequences...
evidence.subsection: The airline industry is subject to extensive government regulation, which is costly and could materially adversely affect our business.

evidence.text: The compromise of our or our business partners’ or service providers’ technology systems resulting in the loss, disclosure, misappropriation of, or access to, our information or that of our customers, employees or business partners could result in legal claims or proceedings, liability or regulatory penalties under laws protecting the privacy and security of personal information, disruption to our operations and damage to our reputation, any or all of which could adversely affect our business.
evidence.subsection: We are at risk of losses and adverse publicity stemming from a serious accident involving our aircraft or aircraft of our airline partners.

evidence.text: If Monroe were to incur a significant liability for which it is not fully insured or for which insurance companies do not or are unable to provide coverage, this could have a material adverse effect on our consolidated financial results of operations or consolidated financial position.
evidence.subsection: Significant extended disruptions in the supply of aircraft fuel, including from Monroe, could have a material adverse effect on our operations and results of operations.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: High exposure to various litigations and regulatory issues.
